summary: Joel Spolsky announces two things: a new article about how to effect change in an organization when you lack authority, and a switch from Mailman to WhatCounts for managing his email notification list. He describes the pain points of Mailman, particularly the inability to customize unsubscribe links per recipient, which forced him to manually process unsubscribe requests for over 7,500 subscribers. The new WhatCounts system automates unsubscription but has the trade-off that replies to notifications no longer reach him directly.
key_points: Even managers can't simply decree change in software organizations — managing developers is notoriously difficult
Mailman's inability to include per-recipient information in bulk emails created a painful manual unsubscribe workflow
Switching to WhatCounts solved list management but introduced a new friction: replies to notifications are silently consumed as bounces
Tolerating bad tooling too long is a common trap, even for someone who writes about software management
money_quotes: Managing developers is a lot like herding cats, only not as fun.
Merely saying 'make it so' doesn't make it so.
If you're just a grunt programmer at the bottom of the totem pole, you can't exactly order people to start creating schedules or bug databases.
Why I put up with this for so long is completely beyond me.
Sometimes you don't have the power to create change in your organization by executive fiat.
key_insight: Effecting change in organizations requires more than authority, and tolerating broken tools too long is a trap even experienced practitioners fall into.
